extra.reasoning:
The property GLOBALLY(opted_out implies not sent) is VIOLATED by the t_resume_glitch transition.

Trace through the witness (14 events, within the 16-event bound):
- Initial: opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0
- optout (t_optout fires): opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0
- tick x12 (t_tick fires each time): ticks increments 0->1->...->12 (capped at 12); each tick also sets sent:=false; opted_out remains true (no transition ever resets opted_out to false). After 12 ticks: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=12.
- send_attempt: guard of t_resume_glitch is (opted_out and ticks>=12) = (true and 12>=12) = true, so t_resume_glitch fires and applies sent:=true. Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=12.

This state has opted_out=true AND sent=true, so (opted_out implies not sent) is false, violating the global property.

Note on rule interaction/priority: on send_attempt with opted_out=true and ticks>=12, both t_send_blocked (guard opted_out) and t_resume_glitch (guard opted_out and ticks>=12) are enabled. The spec defines no priority ordering, so this is nondeterministic. The temporal property must hold on EVERY reachable execution; since at least one resolution fires t_resume_glitch (a legitimately enabled transition), a violating execution exists. The "resume glitch" rule is precisely the defect — it re-enables sending to an opted-out recipient once the tick counter saturates at 12, which is the clinical safety hazard the property is meant to forbid.
